mandarin.breakdown: Formal morning greeting. Used until around 10–11am.
Formal farewell, often used when parting for a long time.
Standard daytime greeting, used from late morning to evening.
Casual morning greeting.
Evening greeting.
Evening greeting, used after sunset.
Formal morning greeting.
Daytime greeting.
Said when going to bed or parting late at night.
Formal way to ask 'How are you?' Used with people you don't know well.
Standard positive response to 'How are you?'
Means 'so-so' or 'not bad'.
Polite way to say you're not doing well.
Common casual response meaning 'pretty okay'.
Used to return the same question to the speaker.
Emphatic positive response.
Neutral response.
Expressing mild fatigue politely.

mandarin.hanzi: ありがとうございます
ありがとうございます
mandarin.breakdown: Formal expression of gratitude. Use with strangers and superiors.
Used to get attention, apologize mildly, or express gratitude humbly.
Casual thank you.
Formal response to thanks.
Formal response after being thanked.
Literally 'I have no excuse'. The most formal apology in Japanese.
Used when asking for something, e.g. ordering food or making a request.

mandarin.hanzi: ありがとうございます

words.hanzi: ありがとうございます

mandarin.breakdown: Formal gratitude.
Getting attention.
Basic self-introduction pattern. Replace 田中 with your own name.
Polite way to ask someone's name. お makes it more respectful.
Asking someone's age.
Asking about origin.
Said when meeting someone for the first time.

mandarin.hanzi: よろしくお願いします

options.english: Nice to meet you / Please treat me well

mandarin.hanzi: よろしくお願いします
mandarin.breakdown: Always said at the end of an introduction. Has no direct English equivalent.
Asking about someone's origin country or city.
Asking for a name.
Asking age.
Confirming something. はい alone is 'yes'; そうです adds 'that's correct'.
Polite way to disagree or deny.
Affirmative response.
Uncertain response.
Polite way to say you don't understand.
Strong affirmation. Stronger than just はい.
Uncertain.
Negative.
Means 'I understood' or 'I see'. Commonly used to confirm instructions.
Asking about something nearby. これ = this, それ = that, あれ = that over there.
Question word for location.
Question word for time.
Question word for person.
Question word for reason. どうして is a softer, more conversational alternative.
Used when asking about price or amount.
Asking quantity.
Choosing among items.
Asking 'how' to do something. More practical than なぜ (why).
Basic X は Y です sentence structure. Subject + は + noun + です.
Standard self-introduction sentence pattern.
Same structure with different noun.
Nationality sentence.

mandarin.hanzi: これは本ではありません
mandarin.pinyin: kore wa hon de wa arimasen
mandarin.breakdown: Negative sentence. ではありません = is not. Casual: じゃない.
これ=this (near me), それ=that (near you), あれ=that (far from both).
Expressing likes. が marks the object of liking. 好き is an adjective, not a verb.
Action sentence with を particle.
Describing quality with い-adjective.
Formal morning greeting.

mandarin.hanzi: よろしくお願いします

options.english: Nice to meet you / Please treat me well

mandarin.hanzi: よろしくお願いします
mandarin.breakdown: Said at end of introduction.

mandarin.hanzi: ありがとうございます

words.hanzi: ありがとうございます

mandarin.breakdown: Gratitude expression.
Formal farewell.

mandarin.hanzi: はじめまして、私は山田です。よろしくお願いします。
mandarin.pinyin: hajimemashite, watashi wa yamada desu. yoroshiku onegaishimasu.

options.english: Nice to meet you, I'm Yamada. Please treat me well.

mandarin.hanzi: はじめまして、私は山田です。よろしくお願いします。
mandarin.pinyin: hajimemashite, watashi wa yamada desu. yoroshiku onegaishimasu.

words.hanzi: よろしくお願いします

mandarin.breakdown: Complete self-introduction sequence used in Japan.
Japanese numbers 1-3. These kanji readings are Chinese-origin (音読み).
7 has two readings: なな (nana) in counting, しち (shichi) in formal contexts.
8 is considered lucky in Japan.
9 has two readings: きゅう and く.
10. じゅう (juu) is the standard reading.
4 has two readings. し (shi) sounds like 死 (death), so よん (yon) is preferred.
5 in Japanese.
6 in Japanese.
Numbers 4-6. Note that 4 is read よん (yon) not し (shi) in counting.
8 — はち (hachi). A lucky number in Japan because 八 widens, suggesting prosperity.
Japanese has two number systems. Native: ひとつ、ふたつ...ここのつ. Used for counting objects.
Chinese-origin reading used in most contexts.
Native Japanese 10.
11 = 十(10) + 一(1). Japanese uses additive system like English.
Monday = Moon day. Japanese days follow the 7 celestial elements: 月火水木金土日.
Friday = Gold day.
Saturday = Earth day.
Sunday = Sun day.
Wednesday = Water day.
Standard way to ask the time. 何時 = nanji (what time).
〜時 (ji) means o'clock. 一時=ichiji, 二時=niji, 三時=sanji...
〜分 (fun/pun) means minutes.
Just the number thirty.
半 (han) means 'half past'. 六時半 = 6:30.
Asking the date. 日 (nichi/ka) means 'day of the month'.
Months in Japanese are simply number + 月 (gatsu). 一月=January, 二月=February...
May = 5th month.
September = 9th month.
December 25th. Note: dates use 日 (nichi) but 1st=ついたち, 2nd=ふつか, etc. are irregular.
Core time words. Note: 明日 can also be read あした (ashita) or あす (asu).
来 prefix means 'next'. 来月=raigetsu (next month), 来年=rainen (next year).
先 prefix means 'last/previous'.
今 prefix means 'this/current'.
Means 'very soon' or 'almost'. もう alone = 'already'.

options.english: What time do you wake up?

mandarin.breakdown: に particle marks specific times. 〜時に = at 〜 o'clock.
Stating daily routine with time + に + verb.

options.english: I go to bed at 7 o'clock.

mandarin.breakdown: Going to bed.
Stating the time.
午前 (gozen) = AM, 午後 (gogo) = PM. Used before the time: 午後三時 = 3pm.
Tuesday = Fire day.

mandarin.hanzi: 午後二時半に会いましょう
午後二時半に会いましょう
mandarin.breakdown: 〜ましょう = let's do ~. A common way to make suggestions.
Japanese has separate humble/honorific words for family depending on whose family you discuss.
Key rule: use 父(chichi) for your own father, お父さん(otousan) for someone else's.
Same humble/honorific distinction as father.
General term for brothers and sisters.
Older brother. Japanese distinguishes older/younger siblings (兄/弟, 姉/妹).

mandarin.hanzi: 私には兄が一人います
mandarin.pinyin: watashi ni wa ani ga hitori imasu

mandarin.hanzi: 私には兄が一人います
mandarin.pinyin: watashi ni wa ani ga hitori imasu
mandarin.breakdown: います is used for animate things (people, animals). あります for inanimate objects.
Younger sister (when talking about your own). お妹さん when talking about others'.
Older sister.
Younger brother.
Saying you are an only child. 一人 (hitori) = one person.

options.english: How many siblings do you have?

mandarin.breakdown: 何人 (nannin) = how many people. Counter 人 (nin/hitori/futari...).

options.english: There are three of us siblings.

mandarin.breakdown: Stating how many siblings. 人 counter: 一人(hitori), 二人(futari), 三人(sannin)...
Specifying type of sibling.
〜番目 = ordinal number (first, second, third...).
I have two older sisters. 二人 (futari) is the irregular counter for two people.
Combined term. お祖父さん (ojiisan) and お祖母さん (obaasan) are honorific forms.
Humble forms. おじさん / おばさん are honorific. Note: おじいさん = grandfather.
Cousin (same word for male/female).
Less common but useful family terms.
大 (dai/oo) prefix means 'big'. 大家族 = big family.
Polite way to ask about someone's profession. お makes 仕事 honorific.
Stating occupation: profession + です.
先生 also used as an honorific title.
Very common occupation in Japan.
Many English loan words are used for modern professions in Japanese (katakana).

mandarin.pinyin: kanojo wa se ga takai desu
kanojo wa se ga takai desu
mandarin.breakdown: 背が高い = tall (literally 'height is high'). 背が低い = short.
い-adjective + 人 = describing a person's character.
Opposite of 優しい.
面白い means both funny and interesting.
真面目 (majime) - な-adjective meaning serious or earnest. Used positively in Japan.
Humble form when referring to your own mother.
Common Japanese occupation.
Student.
Teacher.

mandarin.pinyin: watashi no kazoku wa yonin desu
watashi no kazoku wa yonin desu
mandarin.breakdown: の (no) is the possessive particle = 's.
ご飯 means both cooked rice and a meal in general. を marks the object being eaten.
One of Japan's most famous dishes. 寿司 or すし in hiragana.
Chinese-origin noodle dish hugely popular in Japan.
Battered and fried seafood or vegetables.
Miso soup — a staple of Japanese meals.
飲む (nomu) = to drink. を marks what you drink.
水 (mizu) = cold water. お湯 (oyu) = hot water.
牛 (cow) + 乳 (milk). ミルク (miruku) is also used.
Loan word from English.
Loan word from Dutch 'koffie'. Japan has a strong coffee culture.
Expressing preference with 〜が好き. が marks what you like.
Very popular in Japan, especially in winter. Often eaten while watching TV.
Japan is famous for premium strawberry varieties.
Japanese grapes are often large and seedless.
Katakana loan word. Many fruit names in Japanese are written in katakana.
〜ましょう = let's ~. A polite suggestion.
Common vegetable, used in many Japanese dishes.
玉ねぎ — essential in Japanese cooking.
Used in dishes like じゃがバター (potato with butter) and コロッケ.
なす (eggplant) is popular in Japanese summer cooking. Also written 茄子.
The most common way to order food. E.g.: ラーメンをください = I'll have ramen.

mandarin.hanzi: メニューを見せてください
メニューを見せてください
mandarin.breakdown: 〜てください = please do ~. Very useful request pattern.

mandarin.hanzi: お会計をお願いします
mandarin.breakdown: Asking for the bill at a restaurant.

options.english: I'd like to make a reservation.

mandarin.breakdown: Making a reservation request.
Said before eating — expresses gratitude for the meal. No direct English equivalent. ごちそうさまでした is said after.

options.english: Thank you for the meal (after eating)

mandarin.breakdown: Most common food compliment. うまい (umai) is a more casual/masculine version.
辛い means both spicy and salty depending on context. 塩辛い (shiokarai) = salty specifically.
甘い also means naive or lenient when describing people.
Sour taste.
Bitter taste. コーヒーは苦いです = Coffee is bitter.

words.english: thank you for the meal (after eating)

mandarin.breakdown: Said after finishing a meal. Pair with いただきます (before eating).

options.english: Thank you for the meal (after meal)

mandarin.hanzi: ラーメンを一つください
ラーメンを一つください
mandarin.breakdown: Ordering with quantity. 一つ (hitotsu) = one item using native counting.

mandarin.hanzi: ラーメンはおいしいです
mandarin.breakdown: Stating that ramen is delicious.

mandarin.hanzi: ラーメンはありますか？
mandarin.breakdown: Asking if something is available.

mandarin.hanzi: お会計をお願いします
お会計をお願いします
mandarin.breakdown: Essential phrase at restaurants. Can also say 「お勘定をお願いします」.
A very common question in Japanese conversation and job interviews.
Stating a hobby: [hobby] が趣味です.
料理 = cooking / cuisine.
旅行 = travel / trip.

mandarin.hanzi: 音楽を聴くのが好きです
mandarin.pinyin: ongaku wo kiku no ga suki desu
mandarin.breakdown: 〜のが好きです = I like doing ~. の turns the verb phrase into a noun.
見る (miru) = to see / watch. Used for movies, TV, scenery.
〜に行く = go to a place.
作る = to make / create.

mandarin.hanzi: 絵を描くのが好きです
mandarin.pinyin: e wo kaku no ga suki desu
mandarin.breakdown: 描く (kaku) = to draw / paint. 書く (kaku) = to write. Same reading, different kanji.

mandarin.hanzi: スポーツは何をしますか？
mandarin.pinyin: supootsu wa nani wo shimasu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: スポーツは何をしますか？
mandarin.pinyin: supootsu wa nani wo shimasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: スポーツ is a katakana loan word from English. します = to do.
スポーツ + をします = play/do a sport. Ball sports use します not 遊びます.
野球 = baseball, Japan's most popular sport.
空手 = karate (literally 'empty hand').
〜が得意 = be good at ~. Opposite: 〜が苦手 (ga nigate) = not good at ~.

mandarin.hanzi: どんな音楽が好きですか？
mandarin.pinyin: donna ongaku ga suki desu ka?

options.english: What kind of music do you like?

mandarin.hanzi: どんな音楽が好きですか？
mandarin.pinyin: donna ongaku ga suki desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: どんな = what kind of. Used before nouns to ask about type or category.
J-POP = Japanese popular music. Other genres: クラシック (classical), ロック (rock), ジャズ (jazz).

mandarin.hanzi: クラシックが好きです
mandarin.breakdown: Loan word from English 'classic'.
Loan word from English 'rock'.

mandarin.hanzi: ホラー映画は苦手です
mandarin.breakdown: 苦手 (nigate) = not good at / dislike. More nuanced than 嫌い (kirai = hate).

options.english: Horror movies are not for me.
I watch horror movies every day.

mandarin.hanzi: 毎朝シャワーを浴びます
毎朝シャワーを浴びます
mandarin.breakdown: 毎〜 prefix = every ~. 毎日(mainichi)=every day, 毎週(maishuu)=every week.
磨く (migaku) = to brush / polish. Also used for 靴を磨く (polish shoes).
洗う (arau) = to wash.
とかす = to comb.

mandarin.hanzi: 寝る前に本を読みます
mandarin.pinyin: neru mae ni hon wo yomimasu
mandarin.breakdown: 〜前に (mae ni) = before ~. 〜後で (ato de) = after ~.

options.english: I read a book after waking up.
I read a book before sleeping.
I read a book while eating.

mandarin.hanzi: スマホをよく使います

options.english: I use my smartphone a lot.

mandarin.hanzi: スマホをよく使います
mandarin.breakdown: スマホ is short for スマートフォン. よく = often.
Shortened from パーソナルコンピュータ (personal computer).
Loan word from English 'tablet'.
Loan word from English 'internet'.

mandarin.hanzi: SNSをよくチェックします
mandarin.breakdown: SNS = Social Networking Service. Used in Japan for all social media platforms.

mandarin.pinyin: shuumatsu wa nani wo shimasu ka?

options.english: What do you do on weekends?

mandarin.pinyin: shuumatsu wa nani wo shimasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 週末 = weekend. 平日 (heijitsu) = weekday.
と = with (person). 出かける = to go out / head out.
家にいる = to be / stay at home.
買い物 = shopping. に行く = go to do something.
ゆっくり = slowly / at a relaxed pace. 休む = to rest.
Expressing being good at a sport.

mandarin.hanzi: 週末は映画を見たり、音楽を聴いたりします
mandarin.pinyin: shuumatsu wa eiga wo mitari, ongaku wo kiitari shimasu

options.english: On weekends I do things like watch movies and listen to music.

mandarin.hanzi: 週末は映画を見たり、音楽を聴いたりします
mandarin.pinyin: shuumatsu wa eiga wo mitari, ongaku wo kiitari shimasu
mandarin.breakdown: 〜たり〜たりする = lists multiple activities non-exhaustively.
Weekday activity.

mandarin.hanzi: 趣味は特にありません
mandarin.breakdown: 特に = in particular / especially.

mandarin.hanzi: 毎日日本語を勉強します
毎日日本語を勉強します
mandarin.breakdown: 勉強する (benkyou suru) = to study.
で marks means of transport. 電車で=by train, バスで=by bus, タクシーで=by taxi.
Japan's famous high-speed rail network. Reaches 320 km/h.
Underground railway.
Airplane.
通勤 = commuting to work. 通学 (tsuugaku) = commuting to school.
Asking for location: [place] はどこですか?

mandarin.hanzi: まっすぐ行ってください
まっすぐ行ってください
mandarin.breakdown: Giving directions: まっすぐ=straight, 右=right, 左=left, 曲がって=turn.

mandarin.hanzi: 右に曲がってください
mandarin.breakdown: Turn right.

mandarin.hanzi: 左に曲がってください
mandarin.breakdown: Turn left.

mandarin.hanzi: 信号を左に曲がります
mandarin.breakdown: Turn left at the traffic light.

options.english: Turn right at the traffic light.
Turn left at the traffic light.
Go straight past the traffic light.

mandarin.hanzi: コンビニはありますか？
コンビニはありますか？
mandarin.breakdown: コンビニ = convenience store (7-Eleven, FamilyMart, Lawson). Essential in Japan.
Post office. Japan Post (日本郵便) also offers banking services.
Hospital. 病 (byou) = illness.
Police station. 交番 (kouban) = police box (small local police post).
Library. Japanese public libraries are free and well-maintained.

mandarin.hanzi: 日本はアジアにあります
日本はアジアにあります
mandarin.breakdown: 〜にあります = is located in/at. Used for inanimate locations.
Country + 人 (jin) = nationality. 日本人=Japanese, フランス人=French, 中国人=Chinese.
Japanese nationality.
Britain = イギリス (from Portuguese 'inglês').

mandarin.hanzi: ウクライナから来ました
mandarin.breakdown: 〜から来ました = came from ~. Stating your country of origin.

mandarin.hanzi: すみません、道に迷いました
すみません、道に迷いました
mandarin.breakdown: 道 (michi) = road/way. 迷う (mayou) = to get lost / be confused.

options.english: How many minutes does it take to ~?

mandarin.breakdown: かかる = to take (time). Very useful for transit questions.
Asking distance.

options.english: Is there a bus stop near here?

mandarin.hanzi: 近くにバス停はありますか？
mandarin.pinyin: chikaku ni basutei wa arimasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Asking about nearby facilities.
It's ten minutes on foot. 歩いて = walking (te-form of 歩く).

options.english: It's a ten minute train ride.

mandarin.hanzi: 東京まで一枚ください
東京まで一枚ください
mandarin.breakdown: 枚 (mai) = counter for flat things (tickets, paper, plates).
往 = going, 復 = returning. 片道 (katamichi) = one-way.
One-way ticket.
Reserved seat, required for Shinkansen Green Car.
IC cards like Suica and Pasmo are used for transit and convenience store payment.

options.english: I pay with an IC card.
I pay with a credit card.

mandarin.breakdown: Stating transport method.

mandarin.hanzi: 駅まで歩いて何分ですか？
mandarin.pinyin: eki made aruite nanpun desu ka?

options.english: How many minutes on foot to the station?

mandarin.hanzi: 駅まで歩いて何分ですか？
mandarin.pinyin: eki made aruite nanpun desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Common question when searching for accommodation.

options.english: How many stations to the terminus?

mandarin.pinyin: shuuten made nani eki desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Asking about the final stop.

mandarin.hanzi: 東京行きは何番線ですか？
mandarin.pinyin: toukyou yuki wa nanbanme no sen desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Asking about platforms.

mandarin.hanzi: 大阪まで新幹線で二時間半かかります
mandarin.pinyin: osaka made shinkansen de niji kan han kakarimasu

options.english: It takes two and a half hours to Osaka by Shinkansen.

mandarin.hanzi: 大阪まで新幹線で二時間半かかります
mandarin.pinyin: osaka made shinkansen de nijikan han kakarimasu
mandarin.breakdown: 時間 (jikan) = hours. 分 (fun/pun) = minutes.
Japanese currency. Coins: 1円, 5円, 10円, 50円, 100円, 500円. Bills: 1000円, 5000円, 10000円.
100 yen coin.
The largest yen note.
Asking for change. おつり = the change given back after payment.
The most essential shopping phrase. いくら = how much (price).
税込み = tax included. 税抜き (zeinuki) = before tax. Japan's consumption tax is 10%.
Price before tax.
On sale price.

mandarin.hanzi: クレジットカードは使えますか？
mandarin.breakdown: 〜は使えますか = Can I use ~? Some Japanese shops are cash-only.

options.english: Can I use a credit card?

mandarin.hanzi: 試着してもいいですか？
mandarin.pinyin: shichaku shite mo ii desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: 試着してもいいですか？
mandarin.pinyin: shichaku shite mo ii desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 〜てもいいですか = may I ~? Essential pattern for asking permission.
Sizes in Japan: SS, S, M, L, LL. Note Japanese sizes run smaller than European/US.

mandarin.hanzi: もっと大きいサイズはありますか？
mandarin.pinyin: motto ookii saizu wa arimasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: もっと = more. 大きい = big.

mandarin.pinyin: betsu no iro wa arimasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Asking for color options.
〜すぎる = too ~. 大きすぎる = too big. 小さすぎる = too small.

mandarin.hanzi: 赤いシャツをください

options.english: Please give me a red shirt.

mandarin.hanzi: 赤いシャツをください
mandarin.breakdown: Color adjectives: 赤い(akai)=red, 青い(aoi)=blue, 白い(shiroi)=white, 黒い(kuroi)=black.
Yellow. As adjective: 黄色い (kiiroi).
Green. Traffic lights in Japan say 青 (blue) even though they're green.
Purple.
Many modern color names are katakana loan words: ピンク, オレンジ, ベージュ, グレー.

mandarin.hanzi: もう少し安くなりますか？

options.english: Can it be a little cheaper?

mandarin.hanzi: もう少し安くなりますか？
mandarin.breakdown: Bargaining is not common in Japan except at flea markets and some electronics stores.
〜中 = currently doing / in the middle of. セール中 = currently on sale.
売り切れ = sold out / out of stock.
New stock that just arrived.
Since 2020 Japan charges for plastic bags. Common question at checkout.

options.english: Do you have a point card?

mandarin.hanzi: サイズを確認してもいいですか？
mandarin.pinyin: saizu wo kakunin shite mo ii desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: サイズを確認してもいいですか？
mandarin.pinyin: saizu wo kakunin shite mo ii desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 確認する (kakunin suru) = to confirm / check.
ちょうど = exactly / just right. Used for size, time, and amounts.
きつい = tight / strict. すぎる = too ~.
ゆるい = loose / slack.

mandarin.hanzi: この靴は二十六センチです
mandarin.pinyin: kono kutsu wa nijuuroku senchi desu
mandarin.breakdown: Shoe sizes in Japan use centimeters. センチ = centimeter.

mandarin.hanzi: ポイントカードはお持ちですか？
mandarin.pinyin: pointo kaado wa omochi desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: お持ちですか is the polite/honorific form of 'do you have'. Common at Japanese cashiers.

options.english: Do you have a loyalty card?

mandarin.hanzi: 合計で八百円になります
mandarin.pinyin: goukei de happyaku en ni narimasu

mandarin.hanzi: 合計で八百円になります
mandarin.pinyin: goukei de happyaku en ni narimasu
mandarin.breakdown: 〜になります is used at the register to announce the total.
Discount amount.

mandarin.hanzi: 八千円をお支払いください
mandarin.breakdown: Request to pay.

mandarin.hanzi: これをください。カードで払います。
mandarin.pinyin: kore wo kudasai. kaado de haraimasu.

options.english: I'll take this. I'll pay by card.

mandarin.hanzi: これをください。カードで払います。
mandarin.pinyin: kore wo kudasai. kaado de haraimasu.
mandarin.breakdown: Complete shopping sentence. で marks payment method.

mandarin.hanzi: 好きな科目は何ですか？
mandarin.pinyin: suki na kamoku wa nan desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: 好きな科目は何ですか？
mandarin.pinyin: suki na kamoku wa nan desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 好き becomes 好きな before nouns (な-adjective form).
Other subjects: 理科(rika)=science, 英語(eigo)=English, 歴史(rekishi)=history.
Science subject.
Art subject.

mandarin.hanzi: 英語の授業があります
mandarin.breakdown: 授業 (jugyou) = class / lesson. があります = there is (inanimate).

mandarin.hanzi: ホチキスを貸してください
ホチキスを貸してください
mandarin.breakdown: 貸す (kasu) = to lend. 借りる (kariru) = to borrow. ホチキス = brand name used generically.
コピー = copy (loan word). 機 = machine/device.
Loan word from English.
Loan word from English.
会議 = meeting. 室/部屋 = room. 〜室 is more formal.

mandarin.hanzi: 質問してもいいですか？
mandarin.pinyin: shitsumon shite mo ii desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: 質問してもいいですか？
mandarin.pinyin: shitsumon shite mo ii desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Raising hand and asking permission to speak — standard classroom behavior in Japan.

mandarin.hanzi: もう一度言ってください

options.english: Please say it one more time.

mandarin.hanzi: もう一度言ってください
mandarin.breakdown: Essential phrase for language learners. ゆっくり話してください = Please speak slowly.
Please write it.

mandarin.hanzi: もっとゆっくり話してください
mandarin.breakdown: Asking to slow down.
忘れる (wasureru) = to forget. 〜ました = past polite tense.

mandarin.hanzi: どんな仕事をしていますか？
mandarin.pinyin: donna shigoto wo shite imasu ka?

options.english: What kind of work do you do?

mandarin.hanzi: どんな仕事をしていますか？
mandarin.pinyin: donna shigoto wo shite imasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 〜ています = currently doing / ongoing state.
Common Japanese job: 営業 (sales). Others: 経理(keiri)=accounting, 人事(jinji)=HR.
Accounting department.
IT sector.
残業 (zangyou) = overtime. A significant part of Japanese work culture.

options.english: I work a lot of overtime.

mandarin.hanzi: 締め切りはいつですか？
締め切りはいつですか？
mandarin.breakdown: 締め切り = deadline (literally 'closing cut'). 締める = close, 切る = cut.
間に合う = to be in time. Negative: 間に合いません = won't make it.
〜しそう = looks like / seems like something will happen.

mandarin.hanzi: 会議が延期されました
mandarin.breakdown: 延期 = postponement. される = passive form.

mandarin.hanzi: 報告書を提出しました
mandarin.breakdown: 提出する (teishutsu suru) = to submit / hand in.
試験 = formal exam. テスト (tesuto) = more casual test.
合格 = passing an exam. 不合格 (fugoukaku) = failing. 合格おめでとう！= Congratulations on passing!
Not passing the exam.

mandarin.hanzi: 試験は難しかったです
mandarin.breakdown: Past tense of 難しい. い-adjectives: drop い, add かった.

mandarin.hanzi: JLPT N3に挑戦しています
mandarin.pinyin: JLPT N3 ni chousen shite imasu
mandarin.breakdown: JLPT = Japanese Language Proficiency Test. Levels: N5 (beginner) to N1 (advanced).

mandarin.hanzi: 数学の宿題が難しいです
mandarin.pinyin: suugaku no shukudai ga muzukashii desu
mandarin.breakdown: Describing difficulty of homework.

options.english: I will report to the teacher.

mandarin.breakdown: に marks the recipient. 先生に = to the teacher.
聞く = to ask or to listen.
教える = to teach or to tell someone.

mandarin.hanzi: 毎日一時間日本語を勉強しています
mandarin.pinyin: mainichi ichijikan nihongo wo benkyou shite imasu

options.english: I study Japanese for one hour every day.

mandarin.hanzi: 毎日一時間日本語を勉強しています
mandarin.pinyin: mainichi ichijikan nihongo wo benkyou shite imasu
mandarin.breakdown: 〜ています expresses ongoing/habitual action. 時間 (jikan) = hours duration.
〜が痛い = ~ hurts. Body part + が痛いです is the key pain expression pattern.
足 (ashi) means both foot and leg. 手 (te) means hand and arm in casual speech.
目 = eye, 耳 = ear.
口 = mouth, 鼻 = nose (also means flower: 花).
お腹 (onaka) = stomach. お腹が空いた = I'm hungry. お腹が痛い = stomachache.
気分が悪い = feeling bad/sick. 気分がいい = feeling good.
熱 = fever. 熱を測る = take temperature. 平熱 (heinetsu) = normal body temperature.
風邪を引く = to catch a cold.
〜がします = feel / sense ~. Also: 吐き気がします = feel nauseous.
止まる (tomaru) = to stop. 〜ません = negative polite.
A doctor's most common question. Answer: [body part]が痛いです.

options.english: Since when has it been hurting?

mandarin.breakdown: から = from / since. いつから = since when.

mandarin.hanzi: どのくらい痛いですか？
mandarin.breakdown: Asking degree of pain.

mandarin.hanzi: アレルギーはありますか？
mandarin.breakdown: Allergy question at hospital intake.

mandarin.hanzi: 保険証を見せてください
mandarin.breakdown: Japan's universal health insurance (国民健康保険) covers most residents. Always bring your card.

options.english: Please show me your insurance card.

mandarin.breakdown: 薬を飲む = take medicine (literally 'drink medicine'). 薬を塗る = apply medicine.

mandarin.hanzi: 一日三回飲んでください

options.english: Take it three times a day.

mandarin.hanzi: 一日三回飲んでください
mandarin.breakdown: 〜回 (kai) = counter for number of times. 一回=once, 二回=twice, 三回=three times.

mandarin.hanzi: 一日一回飲んでください
mandarin.breakdown: Once daily dosage instruction.

mandarin.hanzi: 食前に飲んでください
mandarin.breakdown: 食前=before meal, 食後=after meal, 食間=between meals.
処方箋 = medical prescription. Take it to 薬局 (yakkyoku) = pharmacy.
嬉しい = happy about something specific. 幸せ (shiawase) = generally happy / blessed.
悲しい = sad. 泣く (naku) = to cry. 悲しくて泣きました = I was so sad I cried.
怒る (okoru) = to get angry. 〜ています expresses ongoing state.
心配する = to worry. ご心配なく = don't worry (polite).
緊張する = to be nervous / tense. Very common in Japanese before presentations or exams.
運動する (undou suru) = to exercise. 〜ています can express habitual actions.

options.english: I go to the gym regularly.

mandarin.breakdown: 通う (kayou) = to commute / attend regularly. Different from 行く (go once).
One-time visit.
やめる = to quit / stop.

mandarin.hanzi: 体重を減らしたいです
mandarin.breakdown: 〜たい = want to ~. 減らす (herasu) = to reduce. 増やす (fuyasu) = to increase.

options.english: I want to check my weight.

mandarin.breakdown: I have a sore throat.
Said to someone who is sick. One of Japan's most warm expressions.

mandarin.hanzi: おめでとうございます

words.hanzi: おめでとうございます

mandarin.breakdown: Used for celebrations.
Encouragement expression.

mandarin.hanzi: 病院に行ったほうがいいですよ
mandarin.pinyin: byouin ni itta hou ga ii desu yo

options.english: You should go to the hospital.

mandarin.hanzi: 病院に行ったほうがいいですよ
mandarin.pinyin: byouin ni itta hou ga ii desu yo
mandarin.breakdown: 〜たほうがいい = you'd better / should ~. Very common advice pattern.

mandarin.hanzi: 今日の天気はどうですか？
mandarin.pinyin: kyou no tenki wa dou desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: 今日の天気はどうですか？
mandarin.pinyin: kyou no tenki wa dou desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: どうですか = how is it? A versatile question pattern.
Weather words: 晴れ=sunny, 曇り=cloudy, 雨=rain, 雪=snow, 嵐=storm.
Cloudy weather.
降る (furu) = to fall (rain, snow). 雨が降る = it rains.

mandarin.hanzi: 今日は蒸し暑いですね
mandarin.breakdown: 蒸し暑い = hot and humid — describes Japanese summer perfectly. ね seeks agreement.

options.english: It's hot and humid today, isn't it?
It's a nice day today, isn't it?

mandarin.hanzi: 傘を持っていきましょう
傘を持っていきましょう

words.hanzi: を持っていきましょう

mandarin.breakdown: 持っていく = to take something along. 〜ましょう = let's ~.
大 (oo/dai) prefix = heavy / big. 大雨=heavy rain, 大雪=heavy snow, 大雨=heavy rain.
Past tense of raining/snowing.

mandarin.hanzi: 明日雪が降るでしょう
mandarin.breakdown: でしょう = probably / likely (forecast language).
台風 = typhoon. Japan is hit by several typhoons each year, mainly in summer/autumn.

mandarin.hanzi: 日本には四つの季節があります
mandarin.pinyin: nihon ni wa yottsu no kisetsu ga arimasu

mandarin.hanzi: 日本には四つの季節があります
mandarin.pinyin: nihon ni wa yottsu no kisetsu ga arimasu
mandarin.breakdown: The four seasons are central to Japanese culture and art: 春夏秋冬 (spring, summer, autumn, winter).
桜 (sakura) = cherry blossom. 花見 (hanami) = cherry blossom viewing — a major spring tradition.
紅葉 (kouyou) = autumn foliage.
寒い = cold weather. 冷たい (tsumetai) = cold to the touch.
梅雨 (tsuyu) = Japan's rainy season in June-July. 梅 = plum, 雨 = rain.

options.english: I have a cat (as a pet).

mandarin.breakdown: 飼う (kau) = to keep a pet. Different from 買う (kau) = to buy — same sound, different kanji!
狐 (kitsune) = fox. Important in Japanese mythology — a shape-shifting trickster spirit.
兎 = rabbit. Associated with the moon in Japanese folklore.
Famous at Nara Park where deer roam freely.
タヌキ = raccoon dog. A beloved animal in Japanese folklore known for shapeshifting. Often seen as statues outside restaurants.

mandarin.hanzi: 富士山は日本一高い山です
mandarin.pinyin: fujisan wa nihonichi takai yama desu

options.english: Mt. Fuji is the tallest mountain in Japan.

mandarin.hanzi: 富士山は日本一高い山です
mandarin.pinyin: fujisan wa nihonichi takai yama desu
mandarin.breakdown: 〜一 (ichi) after a place = the best/tallest/most ~ in that place. 世界一 = number one in the world.
川 = river. Also in place names: 神奈川, 品川. 海 (umi) = sea, 湖 (mizuumi) = lake.
森 = forest. 林 (hayashi) = grove / small forest.
Japan consists of 6,852 islands.
温泉 (onsen) = hot spring bath. A cornerstone of Japanese travel culture.

options.english: I want to get into a hot spring.
I want to see the ocean.

mandarin.breakdown: 度 (do) = degree. Japan uses Celsius. Summer temperatures often exceed 35°C.
寒い = cold weather. 冷たい (tsumetai) = cold to the touch (food, water, hands).
暑い = hot weather. 熱い (atsui) = hot to the touch. Same sound, different kanji!
暖かい = warm weather. 温かい (atatakai) = warm food/drink.
マイナス = minus (below zero). Loan word from English.

mandarin.hanzi: 明日は雨が降るでしょう
mandarin.pinyin: ashita wa ame ga furu deshou
mandarin.breakdown: Weather forecast phrasing.
一番 (ichiban) = the most. 〜が一番好き = like ~ the most.
Winter preference.
Summer preference.

mandarin.hanzi: 今日は涼しくて気持ちいいですね
mandarin.pinyin: kyou wa suzushikute kimochi ii desu ne

options.english: It's cool and feels nice today, doesn't it?

mandarin.hanzi: 今日は涼しくて気持ちいいですね
mandarin.pinyin: kyou wa suzushikute kimochi ii desu ne
mandarin.breakdown: 〜くて connects adjectives. 涼しくて気持ちいい = cool and feels good.

options.english: I relax in the living room.

mandarin.breakdown: で marks where an action takes place. Different from に which marks destination.
台所 = traditional word for kitchen. キッチン (kitchin) = modern loan word.
Japanese homes separate: お風呂 (bath), トイレ (toilet), 洗面所 (washroom).
寝室 = bedroom. 寝る (neru) = to sleep.
玄関 = entryway where you remove shoes. A uniquely Japanese feature — shoes are always removed before entering.

mandarin.hanzi: テーブルの上に本があります
mandarin.pinyin: teburu no ue ni hon ga arimasu

options.english: There is a book on the table.

mandarin.hanzi: テーブルの上に本があります
mandarin.pinyin: teburu no ue ni hon ga arimasu
mandarin.breakdown: Location expressions: 上(ue)=above, 下(shita)=below, 中(naka)=inside, 横(yoko)=beside.
Traditional Japanese closet used to store futons and bedding during the day.
Western-style wardrobe.
本 = book, 棚 = shelf.
座布団 = flat floor cushion used when sitting on tatami. 座る (suwaru) = to sit, 布団 (futon) = bedding.

mandarin.hanzi: エアコンをつけてください

options.english: Please turn on the air conditioner.

mandarin.hanzi: エアコンをつけてください
mandarin.breakdown: つける = turn on. 消す (kesu) = turn off. Both used for lights, TV, appliances.
洗濯 = laundry. 洗濯する = do laundry. 乾燥機 (kansouki) = dryer.
冷 = cold, 蔵 = store, 庫 = storage.
電子 = electronic. レンジ = range (from English).
炊飯器 = rice cooker. An essential appliance in nearly every Japanese home. 炊く (taku) = to cook rice.
掃除する = to clean. 掃除機 (soujiki) = vacuum cleaner.
洗い物 = things to be washed / washing up. 皿洗い (saraai) = washing dishes.
Doing laundry.
Japan has strict garbage sorting rules: 燃えるゴミ=burnable, 燃えないゴミ=non-burnable.
干す (hosu) = to hang out to dry. Japanese futons are aired outside on sunny days.
家 = house, 賃 = fee/wage. 家賃 = rent.
礼金 is unique to Japan — a non-refundable 'thank you' payment to the landlord. Usually 1-2 months rent.
First and last payments.

mandarin.hanzi: 光熱費とインターネット代
mandarin.breakdown: 光熱費 = utilities (electricity, gas, water).

mandarin.hanzi: ペット可の物件ですか？
mandarin.pinyin: petto ka no bukken desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 可 (ka) = allowed / permitted. 禁止 (kinshi) = prohibited. Many Japanese apartments don't allow pets.

mandarin.hanzi: 近くにスーパーがあります
近くにスーパーがあります
mandarin.breakdown: 近く (chikaku) = nearby. 遠く (tooku) = far away.
隣 = next to / neighboring. 隣の部屋 = next room. 近所 (kinjo) = neighborhood.
大家 = landlord. さん = honorific suffix.
管理 = management, 人 = person.
引っ越し (hikkoshi) = moving to a new place. 引っ越す (hikkosu) = to move house.

mandarin.hanzi: 電気を消してください
mandarin.breakdown: 消す (kesu) = turn off / erase. つける (tsukeru) = turn on.
お風呂に入る = to take a bath. Japanese bath culture: wash before getting in the tub.
シャワーを浴びる = take a shower.
Washing face.

mandarin.hanzi: 部屋を片付けなければなりません

options.english: I have to tidy up my room.

mandarin.hanzi: 部屋を片付けなければなりません
mandarin.breakdown: 〜なければなりません = must / have to. One of Japanese's key obligation expressions.
助ける (tasukeru) = to help / save. In an emergency: 助けて！alone is enough.

mandarin.hanzi: 救急車を呼んでください
救急車を呼んでください
mandarin.breakdown: Emergency numbers in Japan: 119 = ambulance/fire, 110 = police.

mandarin.hanzi: 警察を呼んでください
mandarin.breakdown: Police emergency: 110.

mandarin.hanzi: 消防車を呼んでください
mandarin.breakdown: Fire: 119 (same as ambulance in Japan).
火事 = fire (as an emergency). 火 (hi) = fire in general. 火事だ！= Fire! Shout this to alert others.
失くす (nakusu) = to lose something. Japan has an excellent lost-and-found system — items are often returned!
忘れ物 = item left behind. 落とし物 (otoshimono) = dropped/found item.

mandarin.hanzi: 落とし物を拾いました
mandarin.breakdown: 拾う (hirou) = to pick up / find.

mandarin.hanzi: バッグを盗まれました

words.hanzi: バッグを盗まれました

mandarin.breakdown: 〜られました = passive (something was done to me).

mandarin.hanzi: 遺失物取扱所はどこですか？
mandarin.pinyin: ishitsubutsu toriatsukaijo wa doko desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 遺失物 = lost property. Japan's train stations have excellent lost and found offices.

options.english: Where is the lost and found office?

mandarin.hanzi: 道に迷いました。助けてもらえますか？
mandarin.pinyin: michi ni mayoimashita. tasukete moraemasu ka?

options.english: I'm lost. Could you help me?

mandarin.hanzi: 道に迷いました。助けてもらえますか？
mandarin.pinyin: michi ni mayoimashita. tasukete moraemasu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 〜てもらえますか = could you do ~ for me? A polite request.

mandarin.hanzi: この住所に行きたいです

options.english: I want to go to this address.

mandarin.hanzi: この住所に行きたいです
mandarin.breakdown: Showing your destination address on your phone is a common strategy in Japan.

options.english: I want to go to the station.

mandarin.breakdown: Wanting to go to the station.

options.english: I want to go to the hotel.

mandarin.hanzi: ホテルに行きたいです
mandarin.breakdown: Wanting to go to the hotel.

mandarin.hanzi: 地図を見せてもらえますか？

words.hanzi: を見せてもらえますか

mandarin.breakdown: Politely asking someone to show you a map or point something out.

options.english: Could you draw me a map?
Could you show me the map?

mandarin.hanzi: 警察に届けてください

options.english: Please report it to the police.

mandarin.hanzi: 警察に届けてください
mandarin.breakdown: 届ける (todokeru) = to report / deliver. 交番 (kouban) = local police box.

mandarin.hanzi: 盗難届を出したいです

options.english: I want to file a theft report.

mandarin.hanzi: 盗難届を出したいです
mandarin.breakdown: 盗難 = theft. 届 = report / notification.

options.english: I want to make a statement.

mandarin.breakdown: Legal testimony.
Essential if you don't speak Japanese well enough.

mandarin.hanzi: パスポートを無くしました
mandarin.breakdown: Report passport loss to police first, then contact your embassy.

mandarin.hanzi: 救急病院はどこですか？
mandarin.pinyin: kyuukyuu byouin wa doko desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: 救急病院はどこですか？
mandarin.pinyin: kyuukyuu byouin wa doko desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: 救急 = emergency. 救急車 = ambulance. 救急病院 = emergency hospital.

mandarin.hanzi: アレルギーがあります
アレルギーがあります
mandarin.breakdown: Always tell medical staff about allergies. 薬アレルギー = drug allergy.
Informing about current medication.
持病 = pre-existing / chronic condition.
手術 = surgery. 必要 (hitsuyou) = necessary.

mandarin.hanzi: 大使館に連絡してください
大使館に連絡してください
mandarin.breakdown: 大使館 = embassy. 領事館 (ryoujikan) = consulate.

mandarin.hanzi: ウクライナ大使館はどこですか？
mandarin.pinyin: ukuraina taishikan wa doko desu ka?

mandarin.hanzi: ウクライナ大使館はどこですか？
mandarin.pinyin: ukuraina taishikan wa doko desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: The Ukrainian Embassy in Japan is located in Tokyo (Minami-Azabu).

mandarin.hanzi: 日本大使館はどこですか？
mandarin.pinyin: nihon taishikan wa doko desu ka?
mandarin.breakdown: Japanese embassy abroad.
Consulate — handles visa and passport services.

mandarin.hanzi: 通訳をお願いできますか？
mandarin.breakdown: 〜をお願いできますか = May I request ~? Very polite form of asking for a service.

options.english: Can I request a phone call?

mandarin.hanzi: 警察を呼んでください！
mandarin.breakdown: Urgent request. 110 = police, 119 = ambulance/fire in Japan.

mandarin.hanzi: 地震です！机の下に隠れてください
mandarin.pinyin: jishin desu! tsukue no shita ni kakurete kudasai

options.english: Earthquake! Please hide under the desk.

mandarin.hanzi: 地震です！机の下に隠れてください
mandarin.pinyin: jishin desu! tsukue no shita ni kakurete kudasai
mandarin.breakdown: 地震 = earthquake. Japan has frequent earthquakes. Standard safety: drop, cover, hold on.

mandarin.hanzi: 火事です！避難してください
mandarin.breakdown: Evacuation instruction.

options.english: Tsunami warning! Move to higher ground.

mandarin.hanzi: 津波警報！高台に逃げてください
mandarin.pinyin: tsunami keihou! takadai ni nigete kudasai
mandarin.breakdown: 津波 (tsunami) = Japanese word adopted worldwide.

mandarin.hanzi: 大丈夫ですか？何かお手伝いできますか？
mandarin.pinyin: daijoubu desu ka? nanika otetsudai dekimasu ka?

options.english: Are you okay? Is there anything I can help you with?

mandarin.hanzi: 大丈夫ですか？何かお手伝いできますか？
mandarin.pinyin: daijoubu desu ka? nanika otetsudai dekimasu ka?

words.hanzi: 何かお手伝いできますか
words.english: can I help you with anything?

mandarin.breakdown: 大丈夫 (daijoubu) = okay / fine. One of the most versatile words in Japanese — used as yes, no, fine, and don't worry.

scenarios.title: Ordering at a Ramen Shop
scenarios.description: You are sitting at a cozy ramen shop in Tokyo. The chef is behind the counter waiting to take your order.
tasks: Ask what types of ramen they have
Order your ramen and specify toppings
Ask for the bill and pay

phrasebook.hanzi: すみません、何ラーメンがありますか？
phrasebook.pinyin: sumimasen, nani raamen ga arimasu ka?
phrasebook.english: Excuse me, what kinds of ramen do you have?

phrasebook.hanzi: 醤油ラーメンをください
phrasebook.english: I'll have the soy sauce ramen please.

phrasebook.hanzi: 卵を追加できますか？
辛さは普通でお願いします
ここで食べます
お持ち帰りでお願いします
お会計をお願いします
全部でいくらですか？
phrasebook.english: How much is it in total?

phrasebook.hanzi: 現金で払います
ごちそうさまでした

scenarios.title: Checking into a Hotel
scenarios.description: You have just arrived at your hotel in Osaka after a long flight. The front desk staff is ready to assist you.
tasks: Ask about breakfast and check-out time

phrasebook.hanzi: チェックインをお願いします
phrasebook.english: I'd like to check in please.

phrasebook.hanzi: 予約した〇〇です
phrasebook.english: I have a reservation under the name ~.

phrasebook.hanzi: 朝食は含まれていますか？
チェックアウトは何時ですか？
Wi-Fiのパスワードを教えてください
phrasebook.pinyin: Wi-Fi no pasuwaado wo oshiete kudasai
phrasebook.english: Please tell me the Wi-Fi password.

phrasebook.hanzi: 荷物を預かってもらえますか？
部屋まで案内してください
phrasebook.english: Please show me to my room.

phrasebook.hanzi: 禁煙室をお願いします

scenarios.title: Shopping for Souvenirs
scenarios.description: You are in a souvenir shop in Kyoto looking for a gift to bring back home. The shop assistant is happy to help.
tasks: Ask the shop assistant for a recommendation
Ask about the price and if gift wrapping is available

phrasebook.hanzi: お土産を探しています
何かおすすめはありますか？
これはいくらですか？
ギフト用に包んでもらえますか？
phrasebook.pinyin: gifuto you ni tsutsunde moraemasu ka?

phrasebook.hanzi: もう少し安いものはありますか？
phrasebook.pinyin: mou sukoshi yasui mono wa arimasu ka?
phrasebook.english: Do you have something a little cheaper?

phrasebook.hanzi: これをください
カードで払えますか？
領収書をください
試着してもいいですか？
phrasebook.pinyin: shichaku shite mo ii desu ka?

phrasebook.hanzi: 別の色はありますか？
phrasebook.pinyin: betsu no iro wa arimasu ka?
phrasebook.english: Do you have it in another color?

scenarios.title: Asking for Directions
scenarios.description: You are lost near Shibuya station in Tokyo and need to find the subway entrance. A local passerby is willing to help.
tasks: Politely stop someone and explain you are lost
Ask where the nearest subway station is
Ask how long it takes to get there

phrasebook.hanzi: すみません、道に迷いました
地下鉄の入口はどこですか？
phrasebook.pinyin: chikatetsu no iriguchi wa doko desu ka?

phrasebook.hanzi: 一番近い駅はどこですか？
phrasebook.pinyin: ichiban chikai eki wa doko desu ka?

phrasebook.hanzi: 歩いて何分かかりますか？
phrasebook.english: How many minutes does it take on foot?

phrasebook.hanzi: 地図を見せてもらえますか？
phrasebook.english: Could you show me a map?

phrasebook.hanzi: まっすぐ行ってください
右に曲がってください
左に曲がってください
ありがとうございます、助かりました
phrasebook.english: Thank you, you've been a great help.

phrasebook.hanzi: もう一度言ってください
